title: Respect Yourself, Protect Yourself: Latina Girls and Sexual Identity By Lorena Garcia NYU Press. 2012. 232 pages. $24.00 paper, $79.00 cloth
abstract: It's almost one o'clock and the 12 students in my graduate seminar on Gender, Sexuality, and Intimate Relationships are seated around the conference room's long wooden table, ready to discuss this week's monograph: Respect Yourself, Protect Yourself: Latina Girls and Sexual Identity by Lorena Garcia. As is my custom, I start the class by explaining why I assigned this particular reading. “Respect Yourself, Protect Yourself is one of a crop of recent books on adolescent sexuality,” I tell them. “It's a frequently studied topic, not least because gender identity, sexual identity, and sexual activity come to the fore during adolescence.” I further note that the book, which focuses on Latinas, attends to several topics central to the course: gender and sexual agency, sex education, diversity within ethnic groups, mother-daughter relationships. “The book started out as Garcia's dissertation,” I add; they know that I see such books as especially instructive for junior scholars. “Plus,” I confess, “NYU Press commissioned me to review the project very early in the publication process and I wanted to see how it turned out.”
